title: Service Registry
description: Schema for service_registry.yaml used by the Green Room explorer.

team.description: Human-readable team name used in the UI title.

team_id.description: Canonical team identifier. A service is considered team-owned when its owner matches metadata.team_id.

last_updated.description: Date the registry was last updated (YYYY-MM-DD).

maintainers.description: People responsible for keeping this registry accurate.

business_flows.description: Map of business flow keys to flow definitions. A business flow represents a user-facing journey or product capability that spans multiple services.

data_flows.description: Map of data flow keys to data flow definitions. A data flow describes how a specific dataset or event moves through the system from origin to consumption.

services.description: Map of service keys to service definitions.

registryKey.description: A lowercase identifier using only letters, digits, and underscores. Used as the canonical key for services, flows, and teams.

maintainer.description: A person responsible for keeping this registry accurate.

businessFlow.description: A user-facing journey or product capability that one or more services collaborate to deliver.

name.description: Human-readable name for this business flow.

description.description: What this business flow delivers from a user or business perspective.

priority.description: Incident priority tier for this flow (e.g. P1, P2). Used to communicate on-call severity expectations.

stakeholders.description: Teams or groups with a stake in this flow's reliability (e.g. Product, Finance, Legal).

dataFlow.description: Describes how a specific dataset or event travels through the system, from the service that originates it to the service that finally consumes it.

name.description: Human-readable name for this data flow.

description.description: What data this flow carries and why it matters to the business.

business_flow.description: Key of the business flow this data flow supports.

data_type.description:
Broad category of data moving through this flow.
- dataset: A structured collection of records (e.g. database export, ML training set)
- event: A discrete occurrence that happened at a point in time (e.g. user clicked, order placed)
- metric: A numeric measurement sampled or aggregated over time (e.g. CPU usage, request latency)
- config: Configuration or feature-flag data consumed by services at startup or runtime
- auth_token: A credential or token used to prove identity or authorise access

sensitivity.description:
Data sensitivity classification, used to guide access controls and handling requirements.
- public: No access restrictions; safe to expose externally
- internal: Restricted to employees and internal systems; not for external exposure
- confidential: Sensitive business or personal data; access limited to authorised teams
- restricted: Highest sensitivity (e.g. PII, PCI, secrets); strict access controls and audit logging required

freshness.description: Expected latency from event occurrence to availability in the final stage (e.g. real-time, near-real-time, hourly, daily).

stages.description: Ordered list of services and the role each plays in moving this data through the system.

dataFlowStage.description: A single step in a data flow, identifying which service is involved and what it does with the data.

service.description: Key of the service performing this action.

action.description:
The primary incident-facing role this service plays at this stage.
- produces: Originates data/events from an authoritative source
- queues: Buffers or brokers data through queue/stream infrastructure
- processes: Applies business logic (transform, validate, aggregate, enrich)
- stores: Persists durable state (database/object store/index/cache)
- serves: Reads and delivers data to downstream systems or clients
- consumes: Terminal sink with no forwarding to additional stages

format.description: Data format at this stage (e.g. JSON, Avro, Parquet, Protobuf).

notes.description: Any additional context about what happens at this stage.

service.description: A deployable unit in the architecture — a service, application, or platform component.

name.description: Human-readable display name for this service.

description.description: What this service does and why it exists. Keep it to 1–2 sentences.

type.description:
Runtime category of this service. Determines the icon and graph grouping.
- frontend: User-facing interface layer (browser app, mobile client)
- backend: Synchronous API server; handles request/response from clients or other services
- worker: Async background processor; consumes from a queue or runs scheduled jobs; has no synchronous callers and fails silently from the caller's perspective
- datastore: Persistent storage at rest (relational DB, document store, blob storage, search index, cache)
- infrastructure: Platform plumbing not in the request path (message buses, service mesh, observability stack, CI runners)

status.description:
Operational lifecycle stage of this service.
- active: Healthy, serving production traffic under normal SLA and on-call coverage
- experimental: Deployed but not production-grade; not covered by on-call rotation; may break without notice
- migrating: In active transition (data migration, replatforming); behaviour may temporarily differ from steady-state; treat with extra caution during incidents
- deprecated: Still running but scheduled for removal; consumers must migrate to a replacement before the sunset date

upstream.description: Services this service directly depends on at runtime. Only list immediate dependencies, not transitive ones.

business_flows.description: Keys of the business flows this service participates in. Used to compute impact when the service is degraded.

owner.description: Registry key of the team that owns this service. Compare to metadata.team_id to distinguish team-owned services from external dependencies.

runbook.description: URL to the on-call runbook for this service. Should contain triage steps, common failure modes, and escalation guidance.

health_check.description: URL to this service's health or readiness endpoint. Used during incidents to confirm whether the service is up.

port.description: Primary port this service listens on. Useful for local development and network policy reference.

dashboard.description: URL to the observability or metrics dashboard for this service (e.g. Grafana, Datadog). The first place to look when you get paged.

on_call.description: PagerDuty service name, OpsGenie integration name, or escalation policy URL for this service. Used to route alerts and find the right on-call engineer.

incident_channel.description: Primary Slack or Teams channel for incidents affecting this service (e.g. #sre-payments). Where the team coordinates during an outage.

slo.description: Availability target for this service, expressed as a percentage or a URL to the SLO document (e.g. 99.9%, https://slo.example.com/payments). Sets expectations for on-call triage severity.

dependency.description: A runtime dependency on another service declared in the registry.

service.description: Key of the service being depended on.

protocol.description: Communication protocol used (e.g. HTTPS, gRPC, AMQP, PostgreSQL wire).

criticality.description:
How this dependency affects the calling service if it goes down.
- hard: Synchronous dependency; the calling service fails entirely if this is unavailable. Model hard deps carefully — they define your blast radius.
- soft: Optional or async dependency; the calling service degrades gracefully if this is unavailable (e.g. falls back to a default, skips enrichment, queues for retry).
